audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a student inquiring about parking facilities.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For each of the questions, four alternatives are given.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Decide which of the alternatives a, b, c or d best fits what you hear on the tape.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And circle the appropriate letter.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to the example will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: How do you come to the university each day?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Train or bus or do you have a car?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Oh, I always walk.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I haven't got a car, and anyway, I live quite close.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: The woman says she always walks, so C has been circled.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the first conversation, and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: How do you come to the university each day?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Train or bus or do you have a car?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Oh, I always walk.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I haven't got a car, and anyway, I live quite close.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Do you know anything about parking rights on the campus?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: I was wondering whether students are allowed to park their cars on campus or not.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Um. Yes.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I think it's possible for postgraduate students, but not for undergraduate students.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: That doesn't seem very fair.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: No, I suppose not, but there simply isn't enough room on the campus for everyone to park.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Hmm. Do you need a parking permit?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Yeah, I believe you do.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Where do I get that from?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Um. I think you can get a parking sticker from the administration office.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Where's that?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: It's in the building called Block G, right next to Block E.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Block G?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Oh right.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: And what happens to you if you don't buy a sticker?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Do they clamp your wheels or give you a fine?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: No, I think they tow your car away.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Oh, really?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: And then they fine you as well, because you have to pay to get the car back.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: I'd better get the sticker then.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: Where exactly is the administration office again?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: I'm new to this university, and I'm still trying to find my way around.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: You go along Library Road, past the tennis courts on your left, and the swimming pool on your right, yeah, and the administration office is opposite the car park on the left, you can't miss it.
audioscript.speaker: Female student

audioscript.text: So, it's up Library Road, past the swimming pool, opposite the car park.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Right, I'll go straight over there.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Bye, and thanks for the help.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Now you have some time to look at questions 5 to 12.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As you listen to the next conversation, complete the application form in the spaces numbered 5 to 10, and answer the questions numbers 11 to 12.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Yes, I was told to come over here to get a parking sticker.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Is this the right place?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, it is.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Um. Are you a postgraduate student?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Yes, I am.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: OK, well I'll just need to take some details.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Uh. Your name?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Richard Lee, that's spelt L double E.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Richard Lee. And the address?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Flat 13, 30 Enmore Road.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: How do you spell Enmore?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: ENMORE.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: And that's in the suburb of Newport:
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: NEWPORT.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Faculty?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: I beg your pardon?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Uh. Which faculty are you in?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Architecture, the Faculty of Architecture.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Right, and the registration number of your car.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Uh. Let me see, um, LXJ50... oh, no, sorry.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: I always get that wrong.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: It's LJX058K.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: LJX508K.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Ah.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Um. And what make is the car?
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: It's a Ford.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: A Ford.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Well, um, I'll just get you to sign here, and when you've paid the cashier, I'll be able to issue you with a sticker.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Oh, oh, where do I pay?
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Uh. Just across the corridor in the cashier's office.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Oh, but it's, uh, it's 12:30 now, and they close at 12:15 for lunch.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Uh. But they open again at a 2:15 until 4:30.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Oh, and they're not open till 2:15, no.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Uh. When you get your sticker, you must attach it to the front windscreen of your car.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: I'm afraid it's not valid if you don't have it stuck on the window.
audioscript.speaker: Clerk

audioscript.text: Right, I see.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: Thanks very much.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: I'll, um, I'll just wait here then.
audioscript.speaker: Male student

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: How does the woman travel every day? <input=C>

items.title: What are the parking regulations on campus?
If you do not have a parking sticker, the following action will be taken:
options: tow away your car and fine.

items: Which picture shows the correct location of the Administration office? <input>

items.title: Where must the sticker be displayed?

answer_key.correct_answer: window | windscreen | front window | front windscreen | on the front window | on the front windscreen

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a talk given by a guide showing a group of people round a museum.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 13 to 18.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the guide, and complete the notes by filling in the numbered spaces 13 to 18.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Uh. Good morning everyone, and welcome to the Maritime Museum.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Now, before we commence our tour, I'd just like to tell you a little bit about the history of the museum.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: As you can see, it's a very modern building, built in the post modern style, and it was in fact opened by the Prime Minister of Australia.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: In November 1991, it's been designed with a nautical flavor in mind to remind us of our links with the sea, but the museum isn't only housed in this building.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: There are a number of historic ships docked outside in the harbor, which form part of the museum, and which you are also free to visit, and we'll be coming to them shortly.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Ha. I'd just like to point out one or two things of general interest while we're here.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Um. Handicapped toilets are located on this floor, and the door shows a wheelchair.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Uh. The cloakroom where you can hang your coat or leave your bags.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Is just behind us here.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Uh. The education center is on the top floor, and there's a good little library in there, which you might like to use.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Follow the signs to the education center, yeah, you'll see a lot of little green arrows on the wall.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Uh. The green arrows will take you there.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Ha ha ha.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: The information desk marked with the small letter 'i' on your plan is located right here in the foyer.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: So, if you get separated from your friends, I suggest you make your way back to the information desk, because we'll be returning to this spot at the end of the tour.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: All right, now, uh huh, if you look out this window, you should be able to see where the museum's ships are docked.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: If you want to go on a tour of the old ship, the Vampire, she's docked over there, and you should meet outside on the quay.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: However.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: A word of warning, I don't recommend it for the grandmas and grandpas, because there are lots of stairs to climb.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Uh. Right now, um, let's move on.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Oh, I almost forgot to give you the times for that tour.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Now tours of the Vampire run on the hour, every hour.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: All right?
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Now look at questions 19 to 23.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As you listen to the rest of the talk, complete the notes in the numbered spaces 19 to 23.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Uh. Let's take a walk around the museum now.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: The first room we're coming to is the theater.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: This room is used to screen videos of special interest, and we also use it for lectures.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: There is a continuous video showing today about the voyages of Captain Cook, so come back here later on, if you want to learn more about Captain Cook.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Now we're moving along the gallery known as the Leisure Gallery.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Uh. This is one of our permanent exhibitions, and here we try to give you an idea of the many different ways in which Australians have enjoyed their time by the sea.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Um. Surfing, swimming, life saving clubs, uh, that's all very much a part of Australian culture.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: At the end of this section, we'll come to the picture gallery, where we've got a marvelous collection of paintings, all by Australian artists.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: I think you can buy reproductions of some of these paintings in the museum shop, that's well worth a good look.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Uh. Now we're coming to the members lounge.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Now as a member of the museum, you would be entitled to use the Members' lounge for refreshments.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Uh. Membership costs $50 a year or $70 for all the family, so it's quite good value, because entry to the museum is then free.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: And down at the far end of this floor, you'll find the section which we've called passengers and the sea.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: In this part of the museum, we've gathered together a wonderful collection of souvenirs from the old days when people traveled by ship.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: You'll find all sorts of things there, old suitcases, ships' crockery, uh, first class cabins, decorated in the fashion of the day.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Just imagine what it must have been like to travel first class.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Now, I'm going to leave you to walk around the museum on your own for a while, and we'll all meet back again at the information desk in three quarters of an hour's time.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: I hope you enjoy your time with us at the museum today.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: Guide

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So, if you get separated from your friends, I suggest you make your way back to the information desk, because we'll be returning to this spot at the end of the tour.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: All right, now, uh huh, if you look out this window, you should be able to see where the museum's ships are docked.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: If you want to go on a tour of the old ship, the Vampire, she's docked over there, and you should meet outside on the quay.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: However.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: A word of warning, I don't recommend it for the grandmas and grandpas, because there are lots of stairs to climb.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Uh. Right now, um, let's move on.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Oh, I almost forgot to give you the times for that tour.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Now tours of the Vampire run on the hour, every hour.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: All right?
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Now look at questions 19 to 23.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: As you listen to the rest of the talk, complete the notes in the numbered spaces 19 to 23.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Uh. Let's take a walk around the museum now.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: The first room we're coming to is the theater.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: This room is used to screen videos of special interest, and we also use it for lectures.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: There is a continuous video showing today about the voyages of Captain Cook, so come back here later on, if you want to learn more about Captain Cook.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Now we're moving along the gallery known as the Leisure Gallery.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Uh. This is one of our permanent exhibitions, and here we try to give you an idea of the many different ways in which Australians have enjoyed their time by the sea.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Um. Surfing, swimming, life saving clubs, uh, that's all very much a part of Australian culture.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: At the end of this section, we'll come to the picture gallery, where we've got a marvelous collection of paintings, all by Australian artists.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: I think you can buy reproductions of some of these paintings in the museum shop, that's well worth a good look.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Uh. Now we're coming to the members lounge.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Now as a member of the museum, you would be entitled to use the Members' lounge for refreshments.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Uh. Membership costs $50 a year or $70 for all the family, so it's quite good value, because entry to the museum is then free.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: And down at the far end of this floor, you'll find the section which we've called passengers and the sea.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: In this part of the museum, we've gathered together a wonderful collection of souvenirs from the old days when people traveled by ship.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: You'll find all sorts of things there, old suitcases, ships' crockery, uh, first class cabins, decorated in the fashion of the day.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Just imagine what it must have been like to travel first class.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Now, I'm going to leave you to walk around the museum on your own for a while, and we'll all meet back again at the information desk in three quarters of an hour's time.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: I hope you enjoy your time with us at the museum today.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: Thank you.
utterances.speaker: Guide

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

The Education Centre is signposted by <input>
If you lose your friends, meet at the <input>
How often are the tours of "The Vampire"? <input>
The Leisure Gallery shows how Australian culture is influenced by <input>
The Picture Gallery contains pictures by <input>
Cost of family membership of the museum: <input>
"Passengers and the Sea" includes a collection of <input>

answer_key.correct_answer: stairs to climb | lots of stairs

test_text.title: Revenue Management in Airlines, Hotels and Hire Cars

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In this section, you will hear a university student Mark talking to a tutor and another student about a topic he has studied.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now look at questions 24 to 32.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the conversation and answer questions 24 to 32.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, everybody.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Um, oh, ha, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Uh. It's Mark's turn to talk to us today, so Mark, I'll ask you to get straight down to business.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Now following on from what we were discussing last week in Susan's tutorial on approaches to marketing, uh, you were going to give us a quick rundown on a new strategy for pricing, which is now being used by many large companies known as revenue management.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Uh. Before we go on to your actual tutorial paper on sales targets, uh, is that correct?
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Yeah, OK, well.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: So, what exactly is revenue management?
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Well, it's a way of managing your pricing by treating things like airline tickets and hotel rooms, rather more as if they were perishable goods.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Yeah, I just tried to book a ticket yesterday for perth.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: And would you believe there are 3 different prices for the flight?
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: And what was the rationale for that?
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Well, the travel agent said it depended on when you book, and the length of the stay.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Like it's cheap if you stay away for a Saturday night, presumably because this isn't business travel, and even cheaper if you buy a ticket where you can't get a refund if you have to cancel.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: In that case the ticket costs about half the price.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: You wouldn't think it would make that much difference, would you?
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Well it does, and that's basically because the airlines are now treating their seats like a commodity.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: You see, if you want a seat today, then you pay far more for it than if you want it in 3 weeks' time.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: That seems rather unfair.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Well, not really.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: When you think about it, that's just common sense, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Uh. What this actually means is that in the same row of seats on the same flight, you could have three people who have all paid a different price for their tickets.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: And is this just happening in Australia?
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: No, no, it's the same all over the world.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Airlines are able to market a seat as a perishable product with different values at different stages of its life.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Well like mangoes or apples at the market.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Yeah, it's exactly like that.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: The fact is that the companies are not actually interested in selling you a cheap flight! They're interested in selling the seats and flying aeroplanes that are full.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Uh. Mark, uh, why do you think revenue management has come about?
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Well, as far as I can see, there are two basic reasons.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Firstly, because the law has been changed to allow the companies to do this.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: You see, in the past, they didn't have the right to keep changing the prices of the tickets.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: And secondly, we now have very powerful computer programs to do the calculations, and so the prices can be changed at a moment's notice.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: So you mean 10 minutes could be critical when you're buying a plane ticket?
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: And I understand we've almost reached the stage where these computer programs that the airlines are using will eventually be available to consumers to find the best deals for their travel plans from their home computer.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Heavens, what a thought.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: So the travel agent could easily become a thing of the past if you could book your airline tickets from home.
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Are there any other industries using this system?
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: Or is it restricted to the airline business?
audioscript.speaker: Susan

audioscript.text: No, many of the big hotel groups are doing it now.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: That's why the price of a bed in a hotel can also vary so much,
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: depending on when and where you book it.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: It's all a bit of a gamble really.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Yeah, and hire car companies are also using revenue management to set their tariffs, because they're also dealing with a commodity.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: If you like, so the cost of hiring a car will depend on demand.
audioscript.speaker: Mark

audioscript.text: Well, uh, thank you Mark for that overview, that was well researched.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: Now, uh, let's get on with your main topic for today.
audioscript.speaker: Tutor

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the first part of the talk, and complete the table by filling in the numbered spaces 33 to 37.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Welcome to this talk on space management, and today I'm going to look particularly at space management in the supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh. Now since the time supermarkets began, marketing consultants like us have been gathering information about customers' shopping habits.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh. To date, various research methods have been used to help promote the sales of supermarket products.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh. There is for example the simple and direct questionnaire.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Which provides information from customers about their views on displays and products, and then helps retailers make decisions about what to put where.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh. Another method to help managers understand just how shoppers go around their stores are the hidden television cameras that film us as we shop.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: And monitor our physical movement around the supermarket aisles.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Hmm. Where do we start?
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh. What do we buy last?
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: What attracts us, et cetera.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh. More sophisticated techniques now include video surveillance and such devices as the eye movement recorder.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh. This is a device which shoppers volunteer to wear taped into a headband.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: And which traces their eye movements as they walk around the shop, recording the most eye-catching areas of shelves and aisles.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: But with today's technology, space management is now a highly sophisticated method of manipulating the way we shop to ensure maximum profit.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Supermarkets are able to invest millions of pounds in powerful computers which tell them what sells best and where.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Now an example of this is spaceman, which is a computer program that helps the retailer to decide which particular product sells best in which part of the store.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Now, Spaceman works by receiving information from the electronic checkouts where customers pay.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: On how well a product is selling in a particular position.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Spaceman then suggests the most profitable combination of an article and its position in the store.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Now you have some time to look at questions 38 to 42.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As you listen to the rest of the talk, label the diagram by filling in the numbered spaces 38 to 42.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, let's have a look at what we know about supermarkets, and the way people behave when they walk down the aisles and take the articles they think they need from the shelves.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Now here's a diagram of one supermarket aisle and two rows of shelves.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Here's the entrance at the top left-hand corner.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Now products placed here at the beginning of aisles don't sell well.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: In tests, secret fixed cameras have filmed shoppers' movements around a store over a seven-day period.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: When the film is speeded up, it clearly shows that we walk straight past these areas on our way to the center of an aisle.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Items placed here just don't attract people.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: When we finally stop at the center of an aisle, we pause and take stock, casting our eyes along the length of it.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Now products displayed here sell well and do even better if they're placed at eye level, so that the customer's eyes hit upon them instantly.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh. Products here are snapped up, and manufacturers pay a lot for these shelf areas.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Which are known in the trade as hot spots.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Naturally everyone wants their products to be in a hot spot.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: But the prime positions in the store are the ends of the aisles, otherwise known as gondola ends.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Now these stand out and grab our attention.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: For this reason, many new products are launched in these positions, and manufacturers are charged widely varying prices for this privileged spot.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Also, um, the end of an aisle may be used for promoting special offers, which are frequently found waiting for us as we turn the corner of an aisle.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Well, now, eventually, of course we have to pay.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Any spot where a supermarket can be sure we are going to stand still and concentrate for more than a few seconds is good for sales.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: That's why the shelves at the checkout have long been a favorite for manufacturers of chocolates.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: Perhaps the most sure fire impulse food of all.
audioscript.speaker: Marketing Consultant

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Items placed here just don't attract people.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: When we finally stop at the center of an aisle, we pause and take stock, casting our eyes along the length of it.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Now products displayed here sell well and do even better if they're placed at eye level, so that the customer's eyes hit upon them instantly.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Uh. Products here are snapped up, and manufacturers pay a lot for these shelf areas.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Which are known in the trade as hot spots.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Naturally everyone wants their products to be in a hot spot.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Hmm.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: But the prime positions in the store are the ends of the aisles, otherwise known as gondola ends.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Now these stand out and grab our attention.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: For this reason, many new products are launched in these positions, and manufacturers are charged widely varying prices for this privileged spot.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Also, um, the end of an aisle may be used for promoting special offers, which are frequently found waiting for us as we turn the corner of an aisle.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Well, now, eventually, of course we have to pay.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Uh huh.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Any spot where a supermarket can be sure we are going to stand still and concentrate for more than a few seconds is good for sales.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: That's why the shelves at the checkout have long been a favorite for manufacturers of chocolates.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: Perhaps the most sure fire impulse food of all.
utterances.speaker: Marketing Consultant

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

the most eye-catching areas of the shop
the best <input> for an article in the shop
